ClinchSparkleBiscuit 2025-11-21 2:47 p.m.
Interesting Results: I switch Golden Gate and Old Millard to a spread factor of 8 for a trace path test. I got a 50% (3 of 6) success rate on a trace route compared to a spread factor of 7 where I had failed 20 of 20 attempts. (edited)
1
nightcrawler 2025-11-21 3:34 p.m.
MT LongFast has a link budget of 153dB with data rate of 1.07kbps. MC recommend preset as has a link budget of 149dB with a data rate of 2.73kbps. Increasing it to SF 8 gives it a link budget of 152dB and 1.56kbps. The "deprecated" preset in MC matches MTs LongFast.
nightcrawler
MT LongFast has a link budget of 153dB with data rate of 1.07kbps. MC recommend preset as has a link budget of 149dB with a data rate of 2.73kbps. Increasing it to SF 8 gives it a link budget of 152dB and 1.56kbps. The "deprecated" preset in MC matches MTs LongFast.
nightcrawler 2025-11-21 3:36 p.m.
Page I used for those numbers - https://unsigned.io/understanding-lora-parameters/
LoRa bitrate calculator and understanding LoRa parameters
It can be pretty useful to know how LoRa parameters influence data rate and range, for example when configuring your RNode. In this post I'll go over the basic concepts needed to understand the different LoRa parameters, and how they affect range and data rate.
ClinchSparkleBiscuit 2025-11-21 3:36 p.m.
I am not understanding the link budget thing. But as I understand it, the better the sensitivity the better. Also, I have been taking a gander of the 900 ISM band and it is noisy and jammed packed AF. I think increased bandwidth and spreadfactor would definitlely do well in the noisey band.
ClinchSparkleBiscuit
I am not understanding the link budget thing. But as I understand it, the better the sensitivity the better. Also, I have been taking a gander of the 900 ISM band and it is noisy and jammed packed AF. I think increased bandwidth and spreadfactor would definitlely do well in the noisey band.
nightcrawler 2025-11-21 3:38 p.m.
Generally the higher the number the farther the range. If we really wanted to compare like for like, the deprecated preset would give that
nightcrawler
Generally the higher the number the farther the range. If we really wanted to compare like for like, the deprecated preset would give that
ClinchSparkleBiscuit 2025-11-21 3:42 p.m.
I think using the same lora settings as Long Fast would be worth a try on MC. The real problem, I think, was the meshtastic protocol on top of LORA. The flood routing caused congestion and also the repeaters were snagging hops. I am trying things out to see how they go on a couple of repeaters that have a fringe connection. Having fun experimenting.
ClinchSparkleBiscuit
I think using the same lora settings as Long Fast would be worth a try on MC. The real problem, I think, was the meshtastic protocol on top of LORA. [AL0R] Matt
wider bandwidth equates to lower power density.
ClinchSparkleBiscuit 2025-11-21 5:33 p.m.
I thought it was a single carrier that sweeps within a certain bandwidth? …LORA Chirp (edited)
ClinchSparkleBiscuit 2025-11-21 5:49 p.m.
https://m.youtube.com/watch?v=dxYY097QNs0&pp=ygUabG9yYSBjaGlycCBzcHJlYWQgc3BlY3RydW3SBwkJCwoBhyohjO8%3D
Richard Wenner
LoRa CHIRP
[AL0R] Matt 2025-11-21 5:56 p.m.
While it is a single sweep, the bandwidth equates to the chip rate. 62.5khz and sf7 means that we have 488 symbols per sec. If we increased to 125khz, that increases the symbols per sec to 977. This means that as the band width doubles (and/or the SF reduces by 1), the time to send halves. Thus the signal is shorter and more suceptible to interference.
5:56 p.m.
This is also why you increase the SF, to stretch out the chirp. This also in effect stretches out the fec, making it more noise resistant.
5:58 p.m.
While lora isn't "psk" per say, where the chip starts is similar to the phase shift delta from 0 if it were psk. The chip though, starts at a point some increment above the low frequency of the channel, where the bandwidth is divided by the number of chips, and chips are determined by sf and bandwidth.
ClinchSparkleBiscuit 2025-11-21 5:59 p.m.
Yes, but does the bandwidth have any effect on power density?
[AL0R] Matt 2025-11-21 6:00 p.m.
Not as compared to "fm" or "am". But by stretching the length of the symbol, we make it "stronger" (even though there is no actual increase in the density.
6:01 p.m.
That is why we make the (relative) comparison that MT LF (250/11) is "close" to MC 62/7.
ClinchSparkleBiscuit 2025-11-21 6:02 p.m.
So what did you mean by a wider BW means lower power density… just trying to understand concepts.

Pyromaniac3814 2025-11-21 6:02 p.m.

6:06 p.m.
I just hope it work better than the little one lol
ClinchSparkleBiscuit
So what did you mean by a wider BW means lower power density… just trying to understand concepts.
[AL0R] Matt 2025-11-21 6:06 p.m.
Foregoing the symbol timing.... a receiver that has to "hear" a wider bandwidth, hears "more" signal - including signal that isn't yours. Using the sx1262 chipset.... the receiver has a sensitivity rating of -124db for a 125khz channel (sf7) and -121 for a 250khz channel (sf7). Thus the receiver "loses" 3db of sensitivity every time you double the bandwidth. (edited)
1
6:12 p.m.
If we increase the sf to 12 on a 250 channel, we "recover" not only the lost signal, but some extra, moving the sensitivity to -134dbm. We do this though, at the cost of massively reduced throughput. We have stretched the length of the symbols out so long, that it takes 16ms to send 1 symbol. A 10 byte payload takes 450ms of transmit at sf12. At sf7, it takes 21ms. Moving to sf8 = 42ms.
6:16 p.m.
So why do we have wider bandwidths? Remember, Lora was developed for IoT. A real concentrator can listen to multiple channels and multiple bandwidths concurrently. That means that a closer station with stronger signal can transmit a wider signal at a higher rate. This allows a mixture of devices to use a single concentrator. It is similar to cell sites. If yo uare close, you may have 5g/mm connectivity, but as you move away, the phy rate changes based on distance and signal. Just like wifi. In the same room you may be on a 160mhz channel, but as you move away, you may drop to 40 or even 20, dropping from ax rates down to classic "g/n" rates, perhaps as low as 6mbps.
6:17 p.m.
6:18 p.m.
timmo suggested sf8. I'd be onboard with that.
6:19 p.m.
Remember... MC doesn't flood EVERY packet. We already have greatly reduced traffic just from that. Thus comparing raw data rates isn't apples to apples.
[AL0R] Matt
Remember... MC doesn't flood EVERY packet. We already have greatly reduced traffic just from that. Thus comparing raw data rates isn't apples to apples.
timmo_3.14 2025-11-21 6:28 p.m.

We have stretched the length of the symbols out so long, that it takes 16ms to send 1 symbol. A 10 byte payload takes 450ms of transmit at sf12. At sf7, it takes 21ms. Moving to sf8 = 42ms.

amc4550 2025-11-23 5:19 p.m.

Scott Redd
I have not tried tweaking any of the modem settings. If I switch to SF8 is it still on the same mesh network as everybody else? Or do they all need to switch?

Scott Redd 2025-11-24 9:56 a.m.

AstroBob 2025-11-24 10:01 a.m.
I tend to avoid AI for info. Too much garbage. But, that's just my opinion.
10:06 a.m.
I was once debating a guy online about solar panels, and he started supporting his arguments with stuff that I realized was obviously false. I quickly figured out it was AI garbage. Trying to win a debate with AI bot answers == stupid.
10:12 a.m.
This person was one of the MT developers. Go figure.
10:15 a.m.
Sorry about the rant.
Scott Redd
Do companion radios need to match the coding rate with repeaters?
[AL0R] Matt 2025-11-24 10:16 a.m.
No, devices can specify their own settings.
10:16 a.m.
I'd say that "they" are expecting repeaters to have higher power, thus the need for less fec.
10:19 a.m.
However... I'm thinking this may be geo specific. In theory, the companion is transmitting the higher fec to the repeater, since the repeater has more noise. The repeater potentially has higher power, thus requires less fec to the companion. However, that ignores that there may be elevate noise at the companion, making this moot.
10:22 a.m.
Asymmetric coding rates is fine.
[AL0R] Matt
However... I'm thinking this may be geo specific. In theory, the companion is transmitting the higher fec to the repeater, since the repeater has more noise. The repeater potentially has higher power, thus requires less fec to the companion. However, that ignores that there may be elevate noise at the companion, making this moot.
Scott Redd 2025-11-24 10:23 a.m.

Scott Redd 2025-11-24 2:16 p.m.
Interesting. Tagging @Mike / N2QDK to see if he can help us understand if or how this is supposed to work. Seems like persistent messages (like a BBS) would be the point of room servers, but maybe I am missing something.
Mike / N2QDK 2025-11-24 2:19 p.m.
good afternoon gents - reading back up on this now
1
2:21 p.m.
A RoomServer is like a BBS
2:21 p.m.
it's basically like a channel but with a cache so folks not online or out of range can see the last x number of messages
2:22 p.m.
it's like the last 32 or 64 messages
1
2:22 p.m.
the limit is a function of airtime, not hardware memory
Scott Redd 2025-11-24 2:23 p.m.
Thanks, Mike. Some of the folks that joined my Room Server did not see the messages that had been previously posted. Is the room server supposed to replay the messages when they join?
Mike / N2QDK 2025-11-24 2:23 p.m.
it can take a fair amount of airtime to unspool that number of messages to a specific user
2:23 p.m.
but the good thing is it's a direct message instead of flood
2:23 p.m.
it's supposed to, I believe
2:24 p.m.
default login is "hello" but a blank password in the main meshcore app will use hello in the background if left blank
2:24 p.m.
if a roomserver is used as a repeater, there's certain repeater functions that aren't available like keeping track of neighbors
2:24 p.m.
it's a nerfed repeater
Mike / N2QDK
but the good thing is it's a direct message instead of flood
Scott Redd 2025-11-24 2:26 p.m.
That's neat
Mike / N2QDK 2025-11-24 2:28 p.m.
overall, roomserver in practice feels a little unfinished
2:28 p.m.
it's quirky
Mike / N2QDK
it can take a fair amount of airtime to unspool that number of messages to a specific user
Scott Redd 2025-11-24 2:28 p.m.
OK. So maybe when users revisit the room they might see the messages later after they get caught up?
Mike / N2QDK
overall, roomserver in practice feels a little unfinished
Scott Redd 2025-11-24 2:28 p.m.

Mike / N2QDK 2025-11-24 2:34 p.m.
so any quirks or rough edges you expereince are likely just that

Brandon-CBS1
I have a question for yall. Im located in Lincoln and am curious if there is a network developed in my area and if not how hard would it be to establish one like yall have in Omaha?
Rusty Chinook 2025-11-24 8:00 p.m.
There are probably 20-30 MT nodes that I see pretty regularly in Lincoln (up from about 6-10 last year), but nothing that I would call a solidly linked mesh. FWIW, I did deploy a MC repeater with a reasonably decent antenna for a couple of days last week. I was a bit discouraged by the much diminished range using the default settings compared to MT LF...sent me down a rabbit hole of checking firmware, hardware, and antennas and side-by-side range comparisons. To be fair, that effort probably wasn't as charged as it could have been... my goals for using the mesh are partially related to the ability to use serial communications to automate sending text, which is not (yet) supported in the pre-compiled MC firmware and companion app. Watching this "experiment" play out in Omaha has been quite enlightening. I do have a couple of solar nodes built and ready to deploy, but as rapidly as things are changing up the road, I'm not in any hurry to put them out just yet. (edited)
1
Brandon-CBS1 2025-11-24 8:38 p.m.

ClinchSparkleBiscuit
So if there is a conflict of first 2 characters of the public key - what is to be done? get a new public key? (edited)
timmo_3.14 2025-11-24 10:23 p.m.
Yes, new keys for 1 of the 2, but only if they are both repeaters and can both be seen by a companion. (It would only be an issue if companion wanted to define a path ( not flood routing) The companion would not have control of which one it went through, and it may not reach the destination. )

I’m interested in some automation, as well. I made an AI powered meshbot for MT, as well as some cloud based metrics gathering. Easy with MT. Not exactly sure how much can be done with MC.
Rusty Chinook 2025-11-25 12:41 a.m.
Yep, MT is easy to interface with python cli for bots and I'm sure other more practical purposes...but that requires some power-hungry hardware on at least one end. It is also fairly easy to use serial RX/TX] (https://meshtastic.org/docs/configuration/module/serial/) to hook up arduinos and other low-power external circuits to a node to pass periodic sensor readings or commands back and forth. It looks like that capability may indeed built into [MeshCore, but I really need to do a deep dive and lurk more in some of the other channels to get a handle on how to make things work.
Serial Module Configuration | Meshtastic
This module is an interface to talk to and control your Meshtastic device over a serial port.
Serial and Network Interfaces | ripplebiz/MeshCore | DeepWiki
This document describes the serial and network interface abstraction layer in MeshCore, which enables external applications to communicate with companion radio firmware through multiple transport mech

[AL0R] Matt 2025-11-25 11:53 a.m.
And when the intent is to support paths of 64 hops.... and we know the downfalls of flood from MT... then this infers "larger" networks. Thus a 2 position hex identifier only yields 256 combinations. Am I crazy?
11:54 a.m.

AstroBob 2025-11-25 12:21 p.m.

ClinchSparkleBiscuit 2025-11-25 12:25 p.m.

MeshCore
TESTING NEW MESSAGE SCOPE FEATURES IN MESHCORE!!!
1
[AL0R] Matt
And when the intent is to support paths of 64 hops.... and we know the downfalls of flood from MT... then this infers "larger" networks. Thus a 2 position hex identifier only yields 256 combinations. Am I crazy?
Mike / N2QDK 2025-11-25 12:41 p.m.
Not exactly. We know the shortcomings of MT's "managed" flood: It often manages to make poor decisions on what messages to flood and when, which compromises real-world effectiveness and reach of public channel messages. MC message delivery tends to be more robust because it's a true flood - and there's work being done to help with the timing of flood message sending with repeaters listening for free airtime and repeater owners being able to txdelay as well as a bit of randomness for when to transmit. MC is also a less chatty protocol overall by design - more free airtime means less potential packet collisions. (edited)
12:44 p.m.
using additional public key decimals beyond the curent two would likely be a breaking change, and is slated for MC v2. But again, it's effectively a display bug only and doesn't impact DM or flood routing
12:46 p.m.
here's a good tool to generate a public/private keypair if you're looking to defined first few decimals: https://gessaman.com/mc-keygen/
Mike / N2QDK
Not exactly. We know the shortcomings of MT's "managed" flood: It often manages to make poor decisions on what messages to flood and when, which compromises real-world effectiveness and reach of public channel messages. MC message delivery tends to be more robust because it's a true flood - and there's work being done to help with the timing of flood message sending with repeaters listening for free airtime and repeater owners being able to txdelay as well as a bit of randomness for when to transmit. MC is also a less chatty protocol overall by design - more free airtime means less potential packet collisions. (edited)

Mike / N2QDK 2025-11-25 12:57 p.m.
lol

12:59 p.m.
just a frustrated MT user thrilled to use and advocate for a very robust alternative with a pretty rad user and dev community supporting it all

ClinchSparkleBiscuit

[AL0R] Matt 2025-11-25 2:55 p.m.
I have not used them. Listed specs are 2-2.5db of loss. Ouch. They pass 890-915MHz. No real point to use this with such loss and an offset band (GSM vs ISM).
1
2:56 p.m.
When shopping SAW devices.... just remember that "low loss" doesn't really exist. These are designed for use "in circuit" where loss isn't a real concern.
[AL0R] Matt
I have not used them. Listed specs are 2-2.5db of loss. Ouch. They pass 890-915MHz. No real point to use this with such loss and an offset band (GSM vs ISM).

not even a bug

[AL0R] Matt 2025-11-25 4:01 p.m.

4:01 p.m.

Mike / N2QDK 2025-11-25 4:02 p.m.
not saying it's super awesome or ideal
4:02 p.m.
and very likely to be addressed in v2
[AL0R] Matt 2025-11-25 4:02 p.m.

Mike / N2QDK 2025-11-25 4:07 p.m.

4:11 p.m.

Mike / N2QDK

[AL0R] Matt 2025-11-25 4:12 p.m.

Mike / N2QDK 2025-11-25 4:12 p.m.
but threatening to turn off repeaters doesn't really move the needle on wider project priorities
Mike / N2QDK
but threatening to turn off repeaters doesn't really move the needle on wider project priorities
[AL0R] Matt 2025-11-25 4:13 p.m.
Chill dude. No one is "threatening." It's a statement. If the product isn't mature enough, it isn't mature enough.
Mike / N2QDK 2025-11-25 4:14 p.m.
I'm chill, offering guidance on how to best shine a spotlight on what's a rough edge for you personally
[AL0R] Matt 2025-11-25 4:14 p.m.
I run things at real sites. I don't have the luxury of alpha testing everything. The "project" simpyl won't get access to "better" sites until it reaches a more mature level.
4:14 p.m.
Neither MT nor MC are designed for "real sites." I accept that.
4:15 p.m.
As such, the "community" suffers due to the needs and wants that people like me have for remote admin capability, that 99.9% of the population don't need.
4:16 p.m.
The community wants "better" "high" sites. I won't slap a node at 1100' AGL as it isn't supportable.
Mike / N2QDK 2025-11-25 4:16 p.m.
remote admin = ?
[AL0R] Matt 2025-11-25 4:18 p.m.
Things like "real" OTA updates. "OTA" is advertised, but requires BT/wifi, as well as physical key press in many cases. That's not something you can do at a real site. Private key changes. Requires serial console access, not available OTA.
4:18 p.m.
Again, these things may come, but they aren't a priority at the moment.
4:19 p.m.
It's a chicken and egg scenario. "We" could have nodes at great locations, making it easier for more users to join, however, the lack of these features (for a very few users), is outweighed by features used my more users.
Mike / N2QDK 2025-11-25 4:19 p.m.
is OTA LoRa firmware updates practical? Not really, unless we're talking a mode that drops to really really short SF and wide bandwidth to maximize data rates
Mike / N2QDK
is OTA LoRa firmware updates practical? Not really, unless we're talking a mode that drops to really really short SF and wide bandwidth to maximize data rates
[AL0R] Matt 2025-11-25 4:21 p.m.
And this is absoltuely doable. MC already supports temp radio configs. Change to a "local" high bw setting, upload, then reboot to standard. Nothing big here.
Mike / N2QDK 2025-11-25 4:21 p.m.
MC already has repeater nodes at commercial sites. And yes, it's still relatively early days for the project - there's a lot of work to be done on user-facing and admin sides, for sure
Mike / N2QDK

You don't say.....

Scott Redd 2025-11-26 10:20 a.m.
I don’t like that the T1000-E companion frequently loses the BLE to my phone. But I did notice an interesting behavior. If disconnected, and a message comes in, the device will chirp. If connected, no chirp. This is an improvement over the long and annoying alert the MT firmware plays. (edited)
Scott Redd
I don’t like that the T1000-E companion frequently loses the BLE to my phone. But I did notice an interesting behavior. If disconnected, and a message comes in, the device will chirp. If connected, no chirp. This is an improvement over the long and annoying alert the MT firmware plays. (edited)

Mike / N2QDK 2025-12-02 7:52 a.m.
The operating premise right now on our little mesh here around upstate/ Albany is that if we’re going to be off the standard/default, might as well do SF9 for a bit more in the link budget.
1
Mike / N2QDK 2025-12-02 8:37 a.m.
We don’t have the density of repeaters nor users/messages yet to need faster packets / smaller SF.
8:40 a.m.
But we’re keeping track of repeater owners and doing some work that will help make coordinated switching to a different SF easier once we get more repeaters online
1
nightcrawler 2025-12-02 9:26 a.m.
I'd be up for trying SF9 for the sake of experimentation. I got my v4s yesterday. I set one up as a repeater (Sunrise). I'm still not able to get out, but that is with the stock included antenna. I have a couple of better antennas on the way.
Mike / N2QDK
We don’t have the density of repeaters nor users/messages yet to need faster packets / smaller SF.
Scott Redd 2025-12-02 9:34 a.m.
We’ve got a lot of users, but most are still on MT. Our area MC repeaters are geographically distant (up to 10, 20, 30 miles in some cases), so a SF that facilitates reliability over speed might help. (edited)
Mike / N2QDK
We don’t have the density of repeaters nor users/messages yet to need faster packets / smaller SF.
Scott Redd 2025-12-02 9:36 a.m.
For our “OmaMesh” I will defer to @[AL0R] Matt , who operates a few well placed, but not always as easy to service, sites. At least SF can be done remotely via LoRa.
Mike / N2QDK 2025-12-02 9:55 a.m.
Good to know! Indeed SF is easier to migrate in a coordinated effort than frequency, which could require a cavity filter or other hardware changes. This is a challenge of the project is seen with the southern California mesh, who choose a seemingly arbitrary frequency on the high end of 33cm band based on availability of bandpass filters of a few users (edited)
9:56 a.m.
Current thinking is that up in the spread factor is a smart first step to help buy a few dB’s where mesh links are marginal
9:58 a.m.
strong preference is to keep as many mesh clusters on the default US/Canada radio settings as possible to help ensure interoperability - it's why the alternate/depricated preset was recently removed from the list of presets
10:00 a.m.
But at the end of the day it comes down to what radio settings work best to achieve strong consistent links across the mesh, and SF7 just isn't a good fit in all areas
Mike / N2QDK 2025-12-02 10:10 a.m.
@[AL0R] Matt have you and @Cisien chatted yet? He has a lot of experience with placing fickle LoRa hardware at commercial tower sites up in the PNW, best practices with mitigating stray RFI and ensuring remote access/remote upgrade even if the LoRa side dies, etc. I'm trying the old ferrites on power and data wires trick this week based on his recent suggestions.
Cisien 2025-12-02 10:11 a.m.
shielded box
1
Mike / N2QDK 2025-12-02 10:18 a.m.
I suppose "stray RFI" is a redundant term
AstroBob 2025-12-02 1:43 p.m.
ferrites never hurt. I keep a big box of 'em handy.
Scott Redd
For our “OmaMesh” I will defer to @[AL0R] Matt , who operates a few well placed, but not always as easy to service, sites. At least SF can be done remotely via LoRa.
[AL0R] Matt 2025-12-02 4:46 p.m.
I am not the "oracle" lol. That being said, if we want to crank it to 9, I'm fine with that. at 8 and 8, we are already at 651bps. Going to 9 and 8 will take us to 366bps. That's getting slowwwww. If we do it, I'd say this is as low as we want to go, and as soon as practical, we should move back to 8 and 8.
4:48 p.m.
For me, I can change Valley, NOMA, PAP, and MEAD in a couple of minutes.
Mike / N2QDK
@[AL0R] Matt have you and @Cisien chatted yet? He has a lot of experience with placing fickle LoRa hardware at commercial tower sites up in the PNW, best practices with mitigating stray RFI and ensuring remote access/remote upgrade even if the LoRa side dies, etc. I'm trying the old ferrites on power and data wires trick this week based on his recent suggestions.
[AL0R] Matt 2025-12-02 4:50 p.m.
I haven't chatted on this particular topic. I have supported plenty of ISM gear, as well as commercial Lora though, as well as LMR and microwave.
1
4:52 p.m.
The sites that I have MC at, I am also aware of existing noise sources. Be aware that "ferrites" come in a variety of mixes for different applications. Don't just slap one on and call it good. What are you trying to suppress and by how much?
4:53 p.m.
It's very rare that the use of a ferrite has any impact in a digital system such as this. If your power is "that" noisy, please deal with the source, rather than trying to mitigate it downstream.

Cisien 2025-12-02 7:56 p.m.
that's probably something to tailor to the request. ideally you use the highest gain antenna that works for that site
[AL0R] Matt
It's very rare that the use of a ferrite has any impact in a digital system such as this. If your power is "that" noisy, please deal with the source, rather than trying to mitigate it downstream.
Mike / N2QDK 2025-12-02 8:19 p.m.
Ferrites being the latest suggestion, on top of known tried and true stuff like shielding and grounding that I’ll be trying. There’s multiple nearby (physical and frequency) radio systems between 870 and 890MHz at -45dB that cavity filters can’t quite knock back. On MC, rx is 6-9 dB deaf than xmit at 22dBm. Open to any and all suggestions for mitigation.
Cisien 2025-12-02 8:31 p.m.
it's all just parts of a complete solution, you need proper filtering and protection from RF getting into the transmitter via the antenna when you're at a high power transmitter site
8:31 p.m.
so isolators can be important
8:32 p.m.
get filtering that is decently narrow, or at least blocking out the local transmitters
8:33 p.m.
an isolator is hard with most of these radios as the tx and rx lines are combined. I had to mod a station g2 to add isolators in the tx path
Mike / N2QDK
Ferrites being the latest suggestion, on top of known tried and true stuff like shielding and grounding that I’ll be trying. There’s multiple nearby (physical and frequency) radio systems between 870 and 890MHz at -45dB that cavity filters can’t quite knock back. On MC, rx is 6-9 dB deaf than xmit at 22dBm. Open to any and all suggestions for mitigation.
[AL0R] Matt 2025-12-02 8:42 p.m.
Since I don't recall you mentioning your setup... what filter are you using and whats the plot look like? And where is -45 measured? at the antenna port? Is it -45 at 9xx? or -45 at 890?

Scott Redd 2025-12-03 11:48 a.m.
Here's an email I sent to folks who contacted me via the OmaMesh.net web site. ---- Hi there! Thanks again for reaching out through OmaMesh.net. Here’s a quick update on what’s been happening locally. Meshtastic & MeshCore Meshtastic is still alive and working great. That said, many local users — including some with access to tower sites and pro gear — have been moving to MeshCore because messaging and ACK reliability tend to be better, especially over multiple hops. If you want to try MeshCore, jump into Public Chat, send a message to any Room Server, or just DM someone on the air. Most folks are happy to respond. MeshBot I’m also running a small MeshBot that can respond to pings, act as a Magic 8 Ball, and list nearby stations it has heard. Feel free to interact with it if you see it. Recommended Radio Settings Local testing shows SF9 works better than the default SF7. To match Omaha settings: Preset: USA/Canada (Recommended) Spreading Factor: 9 If you run a repeater: Coding Rate: 8 Save and reboot These settings have noticeably improved metro-wide consistency. Distance & Performance We’ve seen some impressive paths lately: Nearly 60 miles of reliable multi-hop delivery A confirmed 33-mile hop from Council Bluffs → Mead Interactive chats between downtown Omaha ↔ Elkhorn, often with positive ACKs A fun #tictactoe hashtag channel for playing the game entirely over RF Join the Local Discords Meshtastic Omaha: https://discordapp.com/channels/867578229534359593/1217697721285218314 MeshCore Omaha: https://discordapp.com/channels/1343693475589263471/1439732912672211024 Both are friendly places to meet others and ask questions. Website OmaMesh.net will eventually have more content, so feel free to check in from time to time. If you have questions or want help configuring a device or placing a node, just reply — we’re always glad to welcome new operators. Hope to see you on the air! — Scott OmaMesh.net (often on MeshCore as Red X2) (edited)

[AL0R] Matt
FWIW... one of my gripes.... Mine are stuck at 1.10 until I can go onsite.... which means it will be a long time. I wish there was an OTA or an upgrade process that didn't require holding buttons and applying power.
Rusty Chinook 2025-12-03 10:16 p.m.
You probably already know about this so it may be more of a comment for others looking to do something similar, but I just upgraded a rak connected by USB to a SoC board with wifi located in an awkward location using adafruit-nrfutil: adafruit-nrfutil --verbose dfu serial --package RAK_4631_repeater-v1.11.0-6d32193.zip -p /dev/ttyACM0 -b 115200 --singlebank --touch 1200 I didn't need to push any button for upgrades and could connect via terminal emulator after upgrading without issue. You can also do ESP-based boards with: esptool.py -p /dev/ttyUSB0 --chip esp32 write_flash 0x00000 Heltec_v2_companion_radio_ble-v1.11.0-6d32193.bin Here's some further guidance: https://deepwiki.com/ripplebiz/MeshCore/10.1-installation-and-flashing. Use at your own risk, which, in Matt's case, seems to be substantially elevated . Some handy CLI commands if you can connect to the node over USB using a terminal emulator: https://deepwiki.com/ripplebiz/MeshCore/10.2-basic-configuration (edited)
Installation and Flashing | ripplebiz/MeshCore | DeepWiki
This document provides step-by-step instructions for installing MeshCore firmware onto supported hardware devices. It covers the primary web-based flashing method, the PlatformIO development workflow,
Basic Configuration | ripplebiz/MeshCore | DeepWiki
This page covers the initial configuration steps required after flashing MeshCore firmware to a device. It explains how to set radio parameters, configure node identity, understand default credentials

Either way, the deepwiki definitely provides more details into what can be achieved with meshcore-cli get/set <param> <value>
Pyromaniac3814 2025-12-04 3:36 p.m.
Does coding rate change compatibility with the mesh as a whole? Or can I have whatever coding rate without seperating myself from the mesh?
Pyromaniac3814
Does coding rate change compatibility with the mesh as a whole? Or can I have whatever coding rate without seperating myself from the mesh?
nightcrawler 2025-12-04 3:39 p.m.
You can have a different coding rate and still be on the same mesh. You mainly need to have the same frequency, bandwidth, and spreading factor as everyone else. You'll want to use the smallest CR that you can reliably get away with.
nightcrawler
You can have a different coding rate and still be on the same mesh. You mainly need to have the same frequency, bandwidth, and spreading factor as everyone else. You'll want to use the smallest CR that you can reliably get away with.
Pyromaniac3814 2025-12-04 3:43 p.m.
Sweet, thanks!
[AL0R] Matt 2025-12-04 4:25 p.m.
I was creating some notes on site configs... which I have been lax on for the mesh hobby.
[AL0R] Matt 2025-12-04 4:40 p.m.
Here is the primary pass filter for NOMA, showing the current frequency we are running, along with the higher and lower 3db markers, and finally, marker 3 showing the frequency we were using for MT. Once this is cascaded with a window filter I have, I hope that the spur is reduced enough that this parameter combination works "ok" at this site. (edited)
4:42 p.m.
2
[AL0R] Matt 2025-12-04 4:53 p.m.
I showed the full width of the 900 ISM band for scale. For comparison, when you buy a "full band" band pass filter, the entire width of this graph is passed. I've seen conversations mentioning the "25MHz" and "7 MHz" filters someone on Discord bought... the 25MHZ would be almost the full ISM band. Since this band is SO noisy, there is really no point to even using a filter that passes so much. You really need to dial them in, which just isn't the point of the chinesium products.
4:56 p.m.
The filter above is a 6 5/8" diameter, 20" high model. For 900, it's "big." (It also handles 400w, so just a wee bit more power than we're running.) It's a 3/4 wave model, versus the more normal 1/4 wave versions. The size allows for higher Q (sharper pass skirts.) Conversely, the window filters are 4"x4"... because they are meant to produce a broad pass (low Q.)
[AL0R] Matt
I showed the full width of the 900 ISM band for scale. For comparison, when you buy a "full band" band pass filter, the entire width of this graph is passed. I've seen conversations mentioning the "25MHz" and "7 MHz" filters someone on Discord bought... the 25MHZ would be almost the full ISM band. Since this band is SO noisy, there is really no point to even using a filter that passes so much. You really need to dial them in, which just isn't the point of the chinesium products.

Scott Redd 2025-12-05 10:29 p.m.
This is still very much a POC, but check out this map. It's static, for now, so I have to regen and upload. But it's a start. Note that I an not a coder. Just messing around. https://omamesh.net/mc_nodes_map.html
[AL0R] Matt 2025-12-05 10:50 p.m.
Are you making lines based on neighbor info? Or just guessing?
Scott Redd 2025-12-05 11:20 p.m.
The orange path lines on the OmaMesh map show how my MeshBot's node believes it can reach other nodes on the network. MeshCore doesn't broadcast a global routing table, so each device only knows the best routes from its own perspective. The bot listens to advertisements, DMs, channel messages, and path-related events, then reconstructs the hop sequence (like 0f → 1a → fb) by matching those hop IDs to real nodes that have recently broadcast their location. This gives us a clear picture of how routing looks from a well-connected listening post in the network. Even though these routes come from just one node’s viewpoint, they’re still valuable to the whole community. They reveal which repeaters are currently acting as main forwarders, how distant nodes tie into the Omaha cluster, and when paths shift or degrade—something most users can’t see from the phone app alone. Think of the map as a “routing weather radar”: it isn’t the whole truth everywhere, but it’s an accurate and useful snapshot that helps everyone understand mesh behavior in real time.
Scott Redd
The orange path lines on the OmaMesh map show how my MeshBot's node believes it can reach other nodes on the network. MeshCore doesn't broadcast a global routing table, so each device only knows the best routes from its own perspective. The bot listens to advertisements, DMs, channel messages, and path-related events, then reconstructs the hop sequence (like 0f → 1a → fb) by matching those hop IDs to real nodes that have recently broadcast their location. This gives us a clear picture of how routing looks from a well-connected listening post in the network. Even though these routes come from just one node’s viewpoint, they’re still valuable to the whole community. They reveal which repeaters are currently acting as main forwarders, how distant nodes tie into the Omaha cluster, and when paths shift or degrade—something most users can’t see from the phone app alone. Think of the map as a “routing weather radar”: it isn’t the whole truth everywhere, but it’s an accurate and useful snapshot that helps everyone understand mesh behavior in real time.

One idea would be to distribute a MeshBot script to folks who want to run one. You'd need a dedicated radio, and BLE or USB connection to your computer (desktop, server, raspberry pi). [AL0R] Matt 2025-12-07 10:11 p.m.
Connected to your companion, click on contacts. Your repeater should say "direct" (not flood.) If it says direct, then click the three dots on the right, choose Share, Upload to internet Map. It will ask you to confirm. (edited)
[AL0R] Matt
Connected to your companion, click on contacts. Your repeater should say "direct" (not flood.) If it says direct, then click the three dots on the right, choose Share, Upload to internet Map. It will ask you to confirm. (edited)

amc4550 2025-12-08 12:18 a.m.
I still haven't been able to trace sunrise. I'm a meshcore newbie, but my understanding is that all repeaters rebroadcast the same, so that could be a problem having them both running "repeater" at the same location. You might get a cleaner signal into your companion node and back out of your best performing repeater rather than running 2 repeaters.
12:20 a.m.
Repeater 2 might also be stepping on your companion node's acknowledgement if it didn't RX the first message.
Caboose
Same. I'm a big boring nerd. Mithrandir for the repeater. Radagast for the companion node
Scott Redd 2025-12-08 8:25 a.m.
I see your companion node at omamesh.net/map, but not the repeater. Make sure to send some flood adverts from your companion. I am not sure if you can automate the sending of companion adverts like you can a repeater. Omamesh.net map just shows what it can hear from a listener downtown. (edited)

Scott Redd 2025-12-08 9:21 a.m.
I'm glad you're have a good experience so far! I'd recommend the 60 min zero hop advert and 3 hour flood advert for your repeaters. And for your companion, just send one manually whenever you are using the app and think about it. Traffic is lighter, by design, on MeshCore, and it doesn't really gum things up when you advert. (edited)
amc4550
I still haven't been able to trace sunrise. nightcrawler 2025-12-08 5:24 p.m.
I think the default when it doesn't have a path yet (flood) is 3 attempts and 5 when you have a path set. If auto reset is enabled and it exhausted all 5 attempts for a path, it will reset to flood and try one more time.

nightcrawler 2025-12-12 7:35 p.m.
That's interesting. On paper, the settings we are currently using should be better. I don't think you are the only one who has noticed it not being as good. I'm not necessarily suggesting testing it (but we could), but I would think that using the same radio settings as MT in MC should result in the same range. I wouldn't think MC vs MT would affect it.
7:37 p.m.
I still haven't been able to explain the SNR decrease for my nodes going from SF8 to SF9.
7:38 p.m.

1:10 p.m.
Does sent mean my message actually got out?
[AL0R] Matt 2025-12-14 1:11 p.m.

1:11 p.m.
Sent means your companion sent it. You need at least 1 heard repeat... though it is possible that it got out and your companion just didn't hear it.
Caboose 2025-12-14 1:11 p.m.
Oh. Sent is just a "it was pushed out but nobody hear it yet"
Caboose
Oh. Sent is just a "it was pushed out but nobody hear it yet"
[AL0R] Matt 2025-12-14 1:12 p.m.
More or less
Caboose 2025-12-14 1:12 p.m.
Ah beat me to it. I had hope for a second
[AL0R] Matt 2025-12-14 1:19 p.m.
Here's a great example of "noise" in the metro area. I live less than 100y away from a water plant, and they run a 900 ISM FHSS bridge between the plant and water tower, as well as 900 meter collection from the site. Previously, my yagi aimed right at it. Now, I'm about 110 degrees off axis. You can see on the noise floor graph when I swung the yagi yesterday at 230pm. I still here other devices, but I'm nulling out around 20db of the plant transmitter.
1:22 p.m.
Because it is a hopping system, I can't filter out everything. From my previous investigation, It looks like they have about 40 center frequencies, which means that at least 3 of them overlap on our channel, with one 512KHz signal centered on 910.5MHz.
1:24 p.m.
Here is the last 3 days... showing my wonderful noisy neighbor.
1:25 p.m.
There are plenty of other locations around the metro with similar (and worse) noise floors. If people are hearing you, but you can't hear them, this is just another possibility.
Caboose 2025-12-14 1:29 p.m.
That's pretty crazy. I'm in the bottom of a valley but that's a crazy amount of noise. Are you viewing that in the app or with something less?
[AL0R] Matt 2025-12-14 1:30 p.m.
This comes from an observer paired to the Valley repeater. Unfortunately, today, you can only get info like this (graphically) from a directly paired observer.
1:30 p.m.
It is possible to parse the data out from "any" nodes and the rx logs.
1:32 p.m.
from the app, if you request status from your repeater, it will give you a point in time noise floor. (edited)
Caboose 2025-12-14 1:46 p.m.
Okay. That's pretty cool that you can do that. I'll have to check the app for that too (edited)
1:47 p.m.
This is pretty much a side view of my elevation issues until the new node comes in. Everyone is north of me
1:49 p.m.
[AL0R] Matt 2025-12-14 1:55 p.m.

montazuma 2025-12-14 10:27 p.m.
Welcome! I'm far from the expert but I'll give you my two cents on getting started that I wish I would have realized earlier.
  • Look at getting yourself a repeater device and companion device, having both off the bat makes participating at the start much easier and imho makes it easier to learn what you're doing.
  • A solar powered repeater high up in the air is the ideal, but my advice would be to not fret the ideal out the gate and just get something setup. Even if that first repeater is a Heltec v3 taped to a window running off a wall outlet. From personal experience, your best served to just get started and build up to more.
1

PickleKing 2025-12-17 1:17 p.m.
It looks like 25th and 370 will hit a couple repeaters including nightcrawlers. BUT the elevation profile from 25/370 down to the fire station looks pretty unforgiving. You may need to find someone across the river in the loess hills. Or you could try to befriend the Columban Society down there. They’re in a great spot with tall buildings and they’re a friendly community oriented bunch.

PickleKing 2025-12-17 10:14 p.m.
I don’t know all the exact details but I know I’ve also found the delay in adverts to be tricky when starting up new nodes. Which is very different from my experience with meshtastic. I think you have to wait until both radios have performed an advert. Obviously you can trigger an advert with your companion. But in the case of the repeater you just have to wait for it. It would be nice if a repeater would respond with an advert any time a new companion adverts.
PickleKing
I don’t know all the exact details but I know I’ve also found the delay in adverts to be tricky when starting up new nodes. Which is very different from my experience with meshtastic. I think you have to wait until both radios have performed an advert. Obviously you can trigger an advert with your companion. But in the case of the repeater you just have to wait for it. It would be nice if a repeater would respond with an advert any time a new companion adverts.

amc4550 2025-12-17 10:42 p.m.
Yeah, the performance in Bellevue hasn't been great. I hate to say it, but I've been underwhelmed by meshcore overall. I still like the routing protocol, but the Bluetooth connectivity issues are annoying and all the nodes I've tested have behaved erratically at one point or another. Makes it hard to determine how much is RF/topography problems vs software/hardware related.
PickleKing 2025-12-17 10:47 p.m.
AMC, What hardware are you having Bluetooth trouble on? My raks have been flawless. My tbeam usually takes two tries to connect. But other than that it’s been good. As far as the network, I’ve seen comparable or better results with mesh core through out most of the areas I operate in daily. And given that Bellevue’s really does have difficult topography, I think that’s more likely your issue. But it is hard to be sure. (edited)
10:51 p.m.
The other issue I’ve noticed with 900mhz in general is bad signal when the repeater and companion are both below roof/tree line. It seems like having the repeater above that line by even a little makes a big difference since your companion is pretty much always below a true line of sight with trees and buildings.
PickleKing
I don’t know all the exact details but I know I’ve also found the delay in adverts to be tricky when starting up new nodes. Which is very different from my experience with meshtastic. I think you have to wait until both radios have performed an advert. Obviously you can trigger an advert with your companion. But in the case of the repeater you just have to wait for it. It would be nice if a repeater would respond with an advert any time a new companion adverts.
Scott Redd 2025-12-17 10:56 p.m.
You can log into a repeater as admin and have it send an advert on demand. I do that whenever I log in to check battery, neighbors, etc. (edited)

PickleKing
AMC, What hardware are you having Bluetooth trouble on? My raks have been flawless. My tbeam usually takes two tries to connect. But other than that it’s been good. As far as the network, I’ve seen comparable or better results with mesh core through out most of the areas I operate in daily. And given that Bellevue’s really does have difficult topography, I think that’s more likely your issue. But it is hard to be sure. (edited)
amc4550 2025-12-17 11:11 p.m.
Tbeam, seeed s3, t1000e, and Rak 4130. The connection just isn't as reliable and when reconnecting it rarely shows the messages that came in while disconnected.
PickleKing
The other issue I’ve noticed with 900mhz in general is bad signal when the repeater and companion are both below roof/tree line. It seems like having the repeater above that line by even a little makes a big difference since your companion is pretty much always below a true line of sight with trees and buildings.
amc4550 2025-12-17 11:13 p.m.
One of the things I noticed last time I was testing was that my t1000 inside was picking up some stuff that the roof top repeater (+15ft) was missing. Just a lot of strange behavior.
amc4550
One of the things I noticed last time I was testing was that my t1000 inside was picking up some stuff that the roof top repeater (+15ft) was missing. Just a lot of strange behavior.

PickleKing 2025-12-18 12:33 p.m.
I think a fear of heights is reasonable. For anybody in the group that does want a roof install, I’ve found that a coupler and short piece of pvc pipe allows you to pre-assemble everything and the install is simple. Every house has a 3-4” vent stack. And with another 12” of pipe you have plenty of room to mount a node and small solar panel. Install is easy and can be removed just as easy. And you don’t have to spend much time on the roof fiddling with mounting hardware or power tools.
12:35 p.m.
And if you have an HOA you could even mount the node inside the pipe out of sight. Paint the pipe black and mount your solar panel as flat to the pipe as possible and you’re probably going to fool the nosey HOA president. Just don’t drop your node down the vent. Lol (edited)

nightcrawler 2025-12-18 11:44 p.m.
For a repeater, I think WiFi is only used for OTA updates. You can generally remotely manage everything you need for a repeater over Lora in the app.

Scott Redd 2025-12-19 6:51 a.m.

nightcrawler 2025-12-19 3:56 p.m.
Random information - Golden Gate is my best connection out (3dB SNR) and PAP is my second best (-3dB). It falls off quickly after that. It really highlights why things got so bad for me when GG was down. So, thank you all for putting those repeaters up! Hopefully we can get to the point where we have so many repeaters that we drop our SF back to 7.

nightcrawler
I tested SNR between SF8 and SF9 pinging my local repeater and SF8 shows better SNR of about 2-3dB. I still don't know why it would matter all else being equal.
amc4550 2025-12-19 5:39 p.m.
Yeah I saw the same. Took a bunch of measurements across 3 devices and averaged -1.5db from 8 to 9 and another -1 from 9 to 10. Not sure what to make of that.
amc4550 2025-12-19 5:49 p.m.
I also turned the power way down on a node and threw it in a metal box to simulate a weak connection. No real difference between SF8 & SF9. But SF10 significantly improved reliability. I'm not suggesting we go that route since our current settings are so slow already. But it was interesting. (edited)

nightcrawler 2025-12-19 9:03 p.m.

9:04 p.m.
You can pick an unused hex value from the prefix page and ask it to generate one for you
Caboose 2025-12-19 9:04 p.m.
Oh shoot okay. Was looking online and it shows as an open request https://github.com/meshcore-dev/MeshCore/pull/1015
Add CLI command to regenerate key pair by mattzzw · Pull Request #...
In a situation where repeaters face zero hop key prefix collisions, keys will have to be regenerated. Doing this via the set prv.key command is only possible via physical connection to the repeater...
nightcrawler 2025-12-19 9:05 p.m.
Yeah, you have to be connected via USB to change the private key which isn't convenient

[AL0R] Matt 2025-12-21 3:36 p.m.
So on the topic of the L-Com LPDA.... and to get right to Bob's direct reference to a standard yagi... the regular yagi has a very low bandwidth. This is why the driven element is directly connected to transmit. When we talk log periodic though, it's an entirely different story.
2
PickleKing
I’m surprised they don’t get them cheaper than 10.15 but that’s interesting. My guess is that they’re decently above 5000mah but not 7500. Which means that when they’re on a big sale they’re probably about the same as some cheaper online battery stores from what I’ve seen. But no shipping fee/delay if you go to Menards.
Pyromaniac3814 2025-12-21 3:39 p.m.
They do have a 15000 mah one in the 15 dollar range, they're selling that one at a loss right now of about 4 each
[AL0R] Matt 2025-12-21 3:40 p.m.
"Most" LPDAs are driven from the front, where the shortest element determines the highest usable frequency. LPDA elements are cross phased, which is why there are two "booms". Both booms are "hot" even though only one is connected to the center conductor. While both are also connected to ground, the RF radiates as it is an ac coupled signal, not dc. An LPD only has "3" elements in use at any given time. A director, the driven, and the reflector. The driven element is determined by the resonance at the frequency of interest.
3:41 p.m.
The last element on this yagi is also folded, so that it fits in the cover. This also adds a touch of capacitance, further lowering the usable bandwidth of the antenna.
PickleKing
The MT discord: “Hey why are the MC guys setting up all these noisy MT nodes all of a sudden?”
Caboose 2025-12-21 3:43 p.m.
They just need to have the gospel of Meshcore shown to them
[AL0R] Matt 2025-12-21 3:44 p.m.
As to the magic of timmos mod... it would take a real antenna range to determine how effective it is. LPDAs are very balanced antennas, and adding an odd element may lower the swr at a given frequency, but may also distort the pattern significantly. Additionally, the difference in element diameter can have an impact on overall performance as well. The large diameter elements contribute to wider bandwidth. While we are concerned with a very specific frequency, it may work fine, at the cost of "wide band" performance.
PickleKing

As to the magic of timmos mod... it would take a real antenna range to determine how effective it is. LPDAs are very balanced antennas, and adding an odd element may lower the swr at a given frequency, but may also distort the pattern significantly. Additionally, the difference in element diameter can have an impact on overall performance as well. The large diameter elements contribute to wider bandwidth. While we are concerned with a very specific frequency, it may work fine, at the cost of "wide band" performance.
timmo_3.14 2025-12-21 4:21 p.m.
Guess I will just have to do some side by side comparisons with stock and modified when my Christmas nodes get here! I love playing with this stuff.
[AL0R] Matt 2025-12-21 4:28 p.m.
I just swept one of the one's I received. Over the ISM band, RL was -20 (at 902) to -21 (at 928), with -27 at 910 and the low point at 916 with -36. -20 translates to an swr of 1.2:1. I see no reason to tweak these lol. (edited)

amc4550 2025-12-21 10:40 p.m.
Pretty sure I'm having major AGC (automatic gain control) issues with my Rak repeaters. An old heltec V2 was working reliably. Started over today with a reflashed Rak and it was working awesome for about an hour. Left the house for half the day and came back to it being 95% deaf, wouldn't respond to my mobile node until I was ~100 yards away (was working miles away when I left.) Anyone know how to manage AGC, or would this require custom firmware?
amc4550 2025-12-21 11:07 p.m.
Ok so digging around on different channels, it sounds like this is a fairly widespread problem on SX1262 receivers where some sort of AGC "lockup" can be triggered by a strong lora signal on nearby frequencies. I'm running MC, MT, and Lorawan on the same roof ~10-20ft apart which is probably a factor.
Scott Redd 2025-12-21 11:24 p.m.

amc4550
Ok so digging around on different channels, it sounds like this is a fairly widespread problem on SX1262 receivers where some sort of AGC "lockup" can be triggered by a strong lora signal on nearby frequencies. I'm running MC, MT, and Lorawan on the same roof ~10-20ft apart which is probably a factor.
[AL0R] Matt 2025-12-22 7:33 a.m.
There are two settings that influence agc.... this is the first to try set agc.reset.interval 12
Caboose 2025-12-22 10:34 a.m.
Sorry if I broke your bot

amc4550

[AL0R] Matt 2025-12-22 11:32 a.m.
I don't expect a packet would be missed when we're running SF9. I don't have specific timing info, however, even if a packet is missed, in theory, if you have an agc issue, you'd have missed it anyway. Thus on the retransmit, you'd get it rather than miss it.
11:33 a.m.
I do have it set on NOMA, PAP, and MEAD. I've debated putting a g2 back on Valley and comparing the rssi/noise that my oberserver tallies... perhaps this afternoon....

[AL0R] Matt
I don't expect a packet would be missed when we're running SF9. I don't have specific timing info, however, even if a packet is missed, in theory, if you have an agc issue, you'd have missed it anyway. Thus on the retransmit, you'd get it rather than miss it.
amc4550 2025-12-22 11:44 a.m.

PickleKing 2025-12-22 12:35 p.m.
It’s that quick elevation climb of about 100 feet that then falls off again when going north or west of downtown Bellevue. The only way to beat that with nodes at home/garage height will be to get one on top of the edge of that bowl you’re inside. I bet when you drive west out of town you’re picking up your desired repeaters around the Belleaire elementary school area. Side note, I keep noticing omaha has their schools on really good spots for LOS coms. I suppose they have some kind of district system that relies on LOS. Otherwise your options are something south, preferably well past the base, or something east in the loess hills. Or something taller like I’m working on to the north. Lastly, you could make nice with someone that lives on the rim of the “Bellevue Bowl”. There are houses on a few good spots and there’s stuff like the city pool or event center that would be modestly acceptable.
PickleKing
It’s that quick elevation climb of about 100 feet that then falls off again when going north or west of downtown Bellevue. The only way to beat that with nodes at home/garage height will be to get one on top of the edge of that bowl you’re inside. I bet when you drive west out of town you’re picking up your desired repeaters around the Belleaire elementary school area. Side note, I keep noticing omaha has their schools on really good spots for LOS coms. I suppose they have some kind of district system that relies on LOS. Otherwise your options are something south, preferably well past the base, or something east in the loess hills. Or something taller like I’m working on to the north. Lastly, you could make nice with someone that lives on the rim of the “Bellevue Bowl”. There are houses on a few good spots and there’s stuff like the city pool or event center that would be modestly acceptable.
[AL0R] Matt 2025-12-22 12:55 p.m.
Actually, no. Many schools are on prominences for no purpose, other than personal choice. In decades past, OPS (and other school districts), did and do use microwave between some facilities. I've installed and subsequently removed much of this infrastructure in the area. Fiber of course took over. There are some districts though, that still retain microwave redundancy for critical paths, realizing that our illustrious terra based providers suffer from backhoeitis. Systems started in the 1.6GHz band, then moved to 2, then 5, and finally higher to 80GHz in some locations. (That's generally 1g full duplex, though we did do a 10g hop for one customer.) Today, you can run 8g in 6GHz.... take that you WiFi wannabe's.
12:56 p.m.
I suppose I should have said.. systems started in 900MHz... as we did run some megabit systems WAY back that ran in the 900 ISM band we use for Meshcore today. Some are still operational in Lincoln.
12:58 p.m.
Papillion La Vista had a very extensive microwave network, though the majority of their facilities were not on high points. 5/6GHz, 11, and 23 were used in their network. A mix of TDMA, CSMA, and full duplex, topping out at just 300MBps. (edited)
PickleKing 2025-12-22 1:08 p.m.
Super cool info, Matt, thank you! I kind of wish OPS would stop taking all the good spots around town. But on the bright side, for any parents in the group, your kids could have well connected emergency companions in their backpacks which is pretty neat.
1
montazuma 2025-12-22 1:25 p.m.
That is super cool info! It’s funny here in Syracuse church’s take up a lot of the high points. I’m quasi optimistic I can get one or two to host a node, but time will tell.
AstroBob 2025-12-22 1:31 p.m.
Best of luck to you.
[AL0R] Matt 2025-12-22 1:32 p.m.
On the historical.... in the late 90's, Western Multiplex produce the Lynx and Tsunami point to point radios. The Tsunami GX90 was a 45 megabit full duplex unit that operated in the low 5GHz WiFi (UNII1 and 2) before the outdoor power limits applied. OPS deployed a dozen of these. The last one was removed just a few years ago, however, there are still a few GX90s running in the area. All ran at least 2' dishes, with most using 3's, but Burke had a 4' as did an endpoint at the Lucent building (Western Electric, AT&T, Lecent, Avaya, Commscope, etc.) While most WiFi bridges running outdoors came later and had to observe rather low power limits, when the GXs came out, they did not. The originals ran 36dbm (4W), whereas the later rules lowered this to 10dbm (10mw), and when coupled with a 4' dish (36db), produced an erp of ~12,000 watts
1
1:33 p.m.
I still giggle when uninformed people across the US talk about power limits in the 5GHz bands.
AstroBob
Best of luck to you.
montazuma 2025-12-22 1:35 p.m.
I’d like to think in my mind that if looked at has helping provide the community with a service that comes at no cost to them other then me having occasional access then it should be a slam dunk
2
[AL0R] Matt
On the historical.... in the late 90's, Western Multiplex produce the Lynx and Tsunami point to point radios. The Tsunami GX90 was a 45 megabit full duplex unit that operated in the low 5GHz WiFi (UNII1 and 2) before the outdoor power limits applied. OPS deployed a dozen of these. The last one was removed just a few years ago, however, there are still a few GX90s running in the area. All ran at least 2' dishes, with most using 3's, but Burke had a 4' as did an endpoint at the Lucent building (Western Electric, AT&T, Lecent, Avaya, Commscope, etc.) While most WiFi bridges running outdoors came later and had to observe rather low power limits, when the GXs came out, they did not. The originals ran 36dbm (4W), whereas the later rules lowered this to 10dbm (10mw), and when coupled with a 4' dish (36db), produced an erp of ~12,000 watts
AstroBob 2025-12-22 1:37 p.m.
Fascinating, Matt. I was unaware of all this.
[AL0R] Matt 2025-12-22 1:43 p.m.
Our area is awash in unlicensed usage. Schools, governments, commercial, all spray massive amounts of RF into the sky. The ISM bands are even worse, with so many devices in the 900 space for example. It's a downfall for things like Mesh where we are competing with higher powered deives, better antennas, and usually better placement.
1:44 p.m.
A lot of customers start in unlicensed, then migrate to licensed, for lower noise as well as some protection. As WiFi 7 deploys though, a lot of 6GHZ links are in jeopardy. Lawsuits have already been filed in many areas, but it is a losing battle.
1:45 p.m.
People will complain about rising costs, but their pointless need for "gigabit" to their phone is beyond them.
ClinchSparkleBiscuit 2025-12-22 2:46 p.m.

Rusty Chinook 2025-12-23 1:39 p.m.
Yeah, looks like you can see my MC repeater. It is important to understand that the roles of Meshcore devices are different. Repeaters are basically dedicated devices, and companions are what most people would interact with using your computer or phone. This setup can tend to increase the amount of overall hardware that is required to grow the mesh. The chief benefit, as far as I can tell, is more reliable connections, or probably more precisely, the feedback that reliable network connections exist. I still operate MT as well because MC does not yet have the feature set I find most useful.
too3zln 2025-12-23 1:55 p.m.
Do you think I should switch my t114 over to core? It could be more beneficial than staying on meshtastic. I guess only one way to find out.
too3zln
Do you think I should switch my t114 over to core? It could be more beneficial than staying on meshtastic. I guess only one way to find out.
Rusty Chinook 2025-12-23 2:08 p.m.
I would say that depends on your intended purpose. If you currently have good communication with your intended recipients using MT, they would have to switch along with you. There is a very good chance you won't be able to reach those devices from across town because there are few MC nodes (no others than my own that I am aware of) to repeat your messages. If you are just tinkering with no clear purpose, go for it may allow you to learn about how MC works, test range, etc.

Rusty Chinook 2025-12-23 2:48 p.m.
One thing to know about Meshcore companions is that you need to manually advertise your device to the mesh in order to show up in others' contact list (unlike MT which automatically shows new nodes). Advert -Flood routed will be propagated through repeaters

too3zln 2025-12-23 8:17 p.m.
Just figured I set it to SF7. Can I change my pocket V3 to 7, then remote into the roof to change to 9 so I don't have to climb in the dark?!?
too3zln
Just figured I set it to SF7. Can I change my pocket V3 to 7, then remote into the roof to change to 9 so I don't have to climb in the dark?!?
Rusty Chinook 2025-12-23 8:23 p.m.
Yeah, if roof is SF7, change pocket v3 to SF7, then see if you can find the roof repeater. If you can find it, 'Manage' the repeater (it's under Details, default password is password). While managing repeater, change its settings to SF9, update time, and reboot. Then change pocket v3 back to SF9 after disconnecting from repeater and search for it again.

AstroBob 2025-12-23 8:34 p.m.
SF=9, CR=8 for repeaters, is what we have been using. You don't have to change the CR, but you must change the SF.

Rusty Chinook 2025-12-23 9:45 p.m.
Silly question that may have been answered before: in MT after sending a message in LongFast, a cloud with a check mark is an indication that the message was received by at least one other node. In MC Public channel, sometimes there is a "Heard 1 repeat" with a tick in a filled circle indicating that at least a repeater heard the message. Sometimes there is a "Sent" with a tick in a hollow circle. Does the tick indicate that the radio successfully transmitted and does the hollow circle signify it was not acknowledged? Would I get a "Sent" with a filled circle if the message had reached another node directly without an intervening repeater?

If it's just "sent" it means it wasn't heard by anything

too3zln 2025-12-23 11:48 p.m.
What does this mean?
too3zln
What does this mean?
Caboose 2025-12-23 11:52 p.m.
So Meshcore has kind of a...dumb thing where it looks at the first 2 numbers of a repeaters public key and uses that for route tracing. When 2 repeaters have the same first 2 digits in the key it gets weird about displaying hops. It'll often say something went across multiple different hops at once leading to some confusion
too3zln 2025-12-23 11:54 p.m.
Ah. Thanks.
Caboose 2025-12-23 11:56 p.m.
Let's say for example you're dumb and don't notice. Then this happens
11:57 p.m.
It's me. I'm the dumb guy. It shows me hitting Downtown. No way I can do that on one hop. So I had to reflash my repeater

https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Alexanderson_alternator
Alexanderson alternator
An Alexanderson alternator is a rotating machine, developed by Ernst Alexanderson beginning in 1904, for the generation of high-frequency alternating current for use as a radio transmitter. It was one of the first devices capable of generating the continuous radio waves needed for transmission of amplitude modulated (AM) signals by radio. It wa...

Jonzy 2025-12-24 12:15 p.m.
I had a reason to start playing with MT. It was to set up off grid coms with family. I was unable to achieve my goal over a 12 mile area in the city for many reasons. Too many hops and high usage on the freq seemed to top the list. I tried to move to Medium Slow and got rid of the chatter but couldn't span the distance because of the hills and valleys. MC has appeared to provide a more reliable way for communicating but it does change some of the bells and whistles I liked about MT. With the coordination of the MOB meshers, I think the infrastructure is building well.

amc4550 2025-12-24 12:46 p.m.
Yeah that makes sense. On my Rak without the amp I saw numerous instances of AGC lock from a nearby lora signal ~6mhz away. Then when running through the 2 way amp (which worked well on MT and is working well now on Heltec V2) it was pretty much in constant AGC lock and was still missing a bunch of packets even with a 4 second reset. Any idea if there is a way to completely disable AGC? It will miss any distant signals during strong local interference anyway, so I don't see the point in trying to quiet that down. If it's a receiver longevity issue... these are $30 devices. I'd be willing to roll the dice.

PickleKing 2025-12-24 2:19 p.m.
In my mind, either MT or MC could go on to make changes that put either one into a leading position. Nothing is stopping MT from implementing some alternate features in message routing that makes MC obsolete. But for now, MC is the clear winner for messaging. As far as this group is concerned, we’re suddenly hitting what feels like a critical mass moment. I vote we just keep moving on MC and focus on building the group more than an affiliation to a firmware. Then with a functioning group we could pivot back to MT if something big changes in the future. That’s my 2 cents. But I’m along for the ride. And if core repeaters like MEAD and a couple in omaha were to switch, then realistically I’ll be following due to the coverage.
4
Rusty Chinook 2025-12-24 3:02 p.m.
A different (very much minority) perspective: I had initially purchased LoRa equipment thinking more in terms of IoT type of network than a human-to-human communications network. MT seemed like a ready-built way to piggyback non-critical (ok if occasionally missed) IoT-type data over a free network that doesn't require a license and uses cheap hardware that interacts well with sensors and electronics that can be solar powered. A nice playground for extending the capabilities of arduinos and sensors for messaging non-critical data and for remote control. So from my view, the accessibility of long-range radio for non-critical communication and experimentation with other non-RF low-powered hardware is more important than critical person-to-person communication. MC is still in its early stages, so it will likely develop to incorporate more of that into this hobby. Until then, I will be keeping a foot in both worlds.

2
Rusty Chinook
A different (very much minority) perspective: I had initially purchased LoRa equipment thinking more in terms of IoT type of network than a human-to-human communications network. MT seemed like a ready-built way to piggyback non-critical (ok if occasionally missed) IoT-type data over a free network that doesn't require a license and uses cheap hardware that interacts well with sensors and electronics that can be solar powered. A nice playground for extending the capabilities of arduinos and sensors for messaging non-critical data and for remote control. So from my view, the accessibility of long-range radio for non-critical communication and experimentation with other non-RF low-powered hardware is more important than critical person-to-person communication. MC is still in its early stages, so it will likely develop to incorporate more of that into this hobby. Until then, I will be keeping a foot in both worlds.
[AL0R] Matt 2025-12-24 8:24 p.m.
I'd be fine with MT if it was "more reliable than not"... however, when it's nearly 100% unreliable, that begs the question of why? Don't get me wrong, I've used it in a very tactical environment with ATak and it's great... but if you want packets more than line of sight, it simply sucks.
8:26 p.m.
I'm also interested to see how MT 2.6 fares.... it's supposed to add next hop routing over flooding.

Meaningless 2025-12-25 9:02 p.m.
Ok, so since I am new to this stuff I need some advice. I talked to my uncle who lives on the north east side of Lincoln and he had no problem me putting up a node at his place. I would have regular access to this so I planning a typical solar node that I have seen multiple examples of but if there is any recommendation on specific parts to make it as maintenance free as possible that would be great.
2
9:02 p.m.
Also he has some connections in Waverly and Ashland that might allow me to get a node up extreamly high, like top of the water tower high. These would probably be a very rare chance to access so they would need to have high reliability.
2
Meaningless
Also he has some connections in Waverly and Ashland that might allow me to get a node up extreamly high, like top of the water tower high. These would probably be a very rare chance to access so they would need to have high reliability.
[AL0R] Matt 2025-12-25 9:20 p.m.
Welcome to trying to support a very immature application. I support sites that have rare access as well. It is unfortunate that some of the "better" infrastructure nodes have the worst supportability. You want a node that you can update without pressing buttons, and ideally, one that supports OTA (and that is within range of wifi too.) On top of the water tower makes this all but impossible.
9:20 p.m.
It's a tradeoff. Access to do something "today" versus an amazing update in 6 months that just can't be done.
9:22 p.m.
FWIW, a solar node may sound like a great solution, and it's "cheap." Due to access issues like you describe, I have hardware that is "reachable" - albeit with feedline runs that can be 200' or more. That translates to "not cheap." What I get though, is reachability to do updates without having physical access to the device.
9:23 p.m.
I have access to probably the best two locations in eastern Nebraska with "direct mount antennas." I don't have hardware at either though, as there is zero access for any updates. It might be 2 years before they could be touched again.
9:25 p.m.
The devs for both MC and MT have no interest in building support for these scenarios. There are only a handful of users that have these specific needs. I'd wager money though, that if true OTA updates were possible, that nodes would suddenly appear for either platform at previously impossible locations.
Meaningless 2025-12-25 9:28 p.m.
All good points. I will just have to wait and see if his contacts pan out and to see what the exact details are. Hopefully the Ashland site pans out as I think that would be a major link between Lincoln and Omaha.
[AL0R] Matt 2025-12-25 9:29 p.m.
And don't go all out. "We" have avenues through official channels as well.
Meaningless 2025-12-25 9:36 p.m.
Thats one of the points of it being a mesh network right? Robust network that can absorb individual nodes going down. (edited)
Meaningless
Ok, so since I am new to this stuff I need some advice. I talked to my uncle who lives on the north east side of Lincoln and he had no problem me putting up a node at his place. [AL0R] Matt 2025-12-25 11:06 p.m.
like everything... there is a LOT of variability. Indoor patch cable will degrade in UV, so anything outdoors needs to be protected. You also want physical protection from morons, I mean workers, so now you're to an aerial/armored option. Now you're in the buck a foot range. And you'll need to terminate it.... mechanicals are not recommended in vibratory environments, which the radio side is. Fusion splicer? nah, we'll do epoxy. You also need the ground side. Access "inside" the base of water towers is the same as topside. It's a challenge. They don't smile when you ask to drill a hole lol. So what was the point?
Caboose 2025-12-25 11:09 p.m.
True. It gets expensive fast. Seems like a lot of work for what would be easier to have as a programming fix.
11:10 p.m.
Seems like MC and MT are shooting themselves in the foot with their stubbornness
11:10 p.m.
I can't help but notice that one person (Liam) seems to be involved with both. I'm a bit confused as to his role in it all
[AL0R] Matt 2025-12-25 11:11 p.m.
With as many nodes as there are that are "remote".... I'd really think they'd develop something.

ClinchSparkleBiscuit
I popped one of these little chiclet filters on golden gate. The noise floor went down to -115dB from -95dB. The filter costs me 1.5dB of receive sensitivity though. Gonna see if the receive improves and also if it stops locking up once a week.
amc4550 2025-12-27 12:19 p.m.
Is this the exact model you added to GG? Looks like there is another version with 915 center frequecy that might be a better fit for MC/MT. amc4550 2025-12-27 12:49 p.m.
Looks like the 915 version is essentially the same bandwidth (26 vs 25mhz), just with a center frequency at the middle of the 900 ism band. In theory this should perform better, especially if there's any GSM celluar nearby, or if you intend to run any non-standard MC/MT frequencies at the top of the band.

Ideal for LoRa, GSM/3G, and 915 MHz applications, this bandpass filter allows target frequencies while rejecting others, reducing interference and boosting system performance.

PickleKing 2025-12-27 1:11 p.m.
It looks like it should connect to the broader mesh very well from that location. A couple notes on its location: first, it should connect to PT01 assuming it isn’t too far outside the directional wedge of that antenna. I think you’ll be good though. Second, there’s still a bit of elevation between Little John and the lower part of old town Bellevue. That should be overcome by the fact that it’s a short distance and by any height of another repeater down in Bellevue. But that elevation that impedes the literal line of sight is very near little John which may lead to asymmetric signal strength. Overall that’s a solid location that I think will solve the Bellevue issue.

Rusty Chinook 2025-12-27 7:53 p.m.
Imagine if you were one of those farmers at the top of that hill and you figured out that you could hook up a temperature/humidity sensor to a mesh repeater and stick it near the top of your grain bin for <$100...you know, just as a backup sensor for keeping an eye on moisture. Send yourself hourly data over a private channel to a companion connected to your desktop in your office that's just a bit too far for wifi. And the cherry on top...you've just added a link in a greater communication mesh that covers a much wider geographic area.

Rusty Chinook 2025-12-27 10:00 p.m.
It can be helpful if you try building your own antennas, like yagis and moxons, and want to tune them. A nice piece of kit to have in the toolbox. Be warned, that would put you at the threshold of a rabbit hole that is deep and wide. If you are just looking to see actual real-world performance of antennas you already have in hand, I have found actual field testing to be more helpful, especially, if while out and about doing other things, you can pull off the road to ping yourself and take a few notes .
2
Rusty Chinook

Scott Redd 2025-12-27 11:09 p.m.

Scott Redd 2025-12-28 5:39 a.m.
Your V4 max transmit power is 28 dBm, which is about 630 mW. The V3 and many other boards max power is 22 dBm, which is about 156 mW. 10 dBm is 10 mW, comparatively very low. Standard best practice for most RF systems is to use the lowest amount of power to achieve communications. A well placed repeater should probably use the maximum power possible. A companion that has a good path to a repeater could get by with less. I suspect most people just max out everything. Matt could probably explain this better.
nightcrawler
10dbm might actually be about the same as 22dbm of others since it is 10dbm plus boost.
Scott Redd 2025-12-28 5:43 a.m.
I think Meshtastic power settings were like this, where a TX setting was interpreted differently by different devices. I believe MeshCore firmware understands a device’s capabilities, so TX power settings are accurate. If you’ve got the device connected to a power amplifier, then you’d need to match the radio’s output to what the PA wants.
Brandon-CBS1
Question for anyone in Omaha. I'm about an hour and a half from driving through. Does sending a flood Advert get responses or would I need to send a message. I haven't gotten anything from MC this whole drive from Greenbay WI
Scott Redd 2025-12-28 5:50 a.m.
MeshCore adverts are a one way broadcast. You could try Tools, Discover Nearby Nodes, Discover Repeaters, but I don’t always have luck. Many folks just transmit into the Public channel and look for repeats. In OmaMesh we’ve set up a #test channel so folks can test without cluttering Public. Repeaters will repeat any channel. There’s also an auto responder on #test that will let you know it hears if you send the word “test” or “testing”.
1

We could move to a standardized repeater naming convention on the mesh. It would offer the relevant info, be user friendly, and should still be shortish. We want them all connected to be sure but I doubt you want your omaha info spamming out west of Lincoln when the mesh gets that big. Given our recent growth, that seems worth planning for.
